**AlphaFold** is an [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") (AI) program developed by [DeepMind](/wiki/DeepMind "DeepMind"), a subsidiary of [Alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc."), which performs [predictions of protein structure](/wiki/Protein_structure_prediction "Protein structure prediction").[[1]](#cite_note-1) It is designed using [deep learning](/wiki/Deep_learning "Deep learning") techniques.[[2]](#cite_note-mittr20201130-2)

AlphaFold 1 (2018) placed first in the overall rankings of the 13th [Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction](/wiki/Critical_Assessment_of_Structure_Prediction "Critical Assessment of Structure Prediction") (CASP) in December 2018. It was particularly successful at predicting the most accurate structures for targets rated as most difficult by the competition organizers, where no existing [template structures](/wiki/Threading_(protein_sequence) "Threading (protein sequence)") were available from [proteins](/wiki/Proteins "Proteins") with partially similar sequences.

AlphaFold 2 (2020) repeated this placement in the CASP14 competition in November 2020.[[3]](#cite_note-cnbc20201130-3) It achieved a level of accuracy much higher than any other entry.[[2]](#cite_note-mittr20201130-2)[[4]](#cite_note-Stoddart-4) It scored above 90 on CASP's [global distance test](/wiki/Global_distance_test "Global distance test") (GDT) for approximately two-thirds of the proteins, a test measuring the similarity between a computationally predicted structure and the experimentally determined structure, where 100 represents a complete match.[[2]](#cite_note-mittr20201130-2)[[5]](#cite_note-science20201130-5) The inclusion of [metagenomic](/wiki/Metagenomics "Metagenomics") data has improved the quality of the prediction of [multiple sequence alignments](/wiki/Multiple_sequence_alignment "Multiple sequence alignment"). One of the biggest sources of the training data was the custom-built Big Fantastic Database of 65,983,866 protein families, represented as multiple sequence alignments and [Hidden Markov models](/wiki/Hidden_Markov_model "Hidden Markov model"), covering 2,204,359,010 protein sequences from reference databases, metagenomes, and metatranscriptomes.[[6]](#cite_note-nature20210715-6)

AlphaFold 2's results at CASP14 were described as "astounding"[[7]](#cite_note-AlQuraishiTweet-7) and "transformational".[[8]](#cite_note-:5-8) However, some researchers noted that the accuracy was insufficient for a third of its predictions, and that it did not reveal the underlying mechanism or rules of [protein folding](/wiki/Protein_folding "Protein folding") for the [protein folding problem](/wiki/Protein_folding_problem "Protein folding problem"), which remains unsolved.[[9]](#cite_note-curry-9)[[10]](#cite_note-10)

Despite this, the technical achievement was widely recognized. On 15 July 2021, the AlphaFold 2 paper was published in *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")* as an advance access publication alongside [open source software](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") and a searchable database of species [proteomes](/wiki/Proteome "Proteome").[[6]](#cite_note-nature20210715-6)[[11]](#cite_note-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) As of November 2025, the paper had been cited nearly 43,000 times.[[13]](#cite_note-13)

AlphaFold 3 was announced on 8 May 2024. It can predict the structure of [complexes](/wiki/Protein_complexes "Protein complexes") created by proteins with [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA"), [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA"), various [ligands](/wiki/Ligand_(biochemistry) "Ligand (biochemistry)"), and [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion").[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)[[15]](#cite_note-:8-15) The new prediction method shows a minimum 50% improvement in accuracy for protein interactions with other molecules compared to existing methods.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

[Demis Hassabis](/wiki/Demis_Hassabis "Demis Hassabis") and [John Jumper](/wiki/John_M._Jumper "John M. Jumper") shared one half of the 2024 [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry"), awarded "for protein structure prediction," while the other half went to [David Baker](/wiki/David_Baker_(biochemist) "David Baker (biochemist)") "for computational protein design."[[17]](#cite_note-NobelChemistry2024-17) Hassabis and Jumper had previously won the [Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences](/wiki/Breakthrough_Prize_in_Life_Sciences "Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences") and the [Albert Lasker Award for Basic Medical Research](/wiki/Albert_Lasker_Award_for_Basic_Medical_Research "Albert Lasker Award for Basic Medical Research") in 2023 for their leadership of the AlphaFold project.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-18)[[19]](#cite_note-:7-19)

## Background

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Background")]

See also: [Protein structure prediction](/wiki/Protein_structure_prediction "Protein structure prediction") and [De novo protein structure prediction](/wiki/De_novo_protein_structure_prediction "De novo protein structure prediction")

[![three individual polypeptide chains at different levels of folding and a cluster of chains](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/dd/Protein_folding_figure.png/330px-Protein_folding_figure.png)](/wiki/File:Protein_folding_figure.png)

Amino-acid chains, known as [polypeptides](/wiki/Polypeptide "Polypeptide"), fold to form a protein

[Proteins](/wiki/Protein "Protein") consist of [chains of amino acids](/wiki/Protein_primary_structure "Protein primary structure") which [spontaneously fold](/wiki/Protein_folding "Protein folding") to form the [three dimensional (3-D) structures](/wiki/Protein_tertiary_structure "Protein tertiary structure") of the proteins. The 3-D structure is crucial to understanding the biological function of the protein.

Protein structures can be determined experimentally through techniques such as [X-ray crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography"), [cryo-electron microscopy](/wiki/Cryo-Electron_Microscopy "Cryo-Electron Microscopy") and [nuclear magnetic resonance](/wiki/Nuclear_magnetic_resonance "Nuclear magnetic resonance") (NMR), which are all expensive and time-consuming.[[20]](#cite_note-:3-20) Such efforts, using the experimental methods, have identified the structures of about 170,000 proteins over the last 60 years, while there are over 200 million known proteins across all life forms.[[5]](#cite_note-science20201130-5)

Over the years, researchers have applied numerous computational methods to [predict the 3D structures of proteins](/wiki/Protein_structure_prediction#Ab_initio_protein_modelling "Protein structure prediction") from their amino acid sequences, accuracy of such methods in best possible scenario is close to experimental techniques (NMR) by the use of [homology modeling](/wiki/Homology_modeling "Homology modeling") based on molecular evolution. [CASP](/wiki/CASP "CASP"), which was launched in 1994 to challenge the scientific community to produce their best protein structure predictions, found that [GDT](/wiki/Global_distance_test "Global distance test") scores of only about 40 out of 100 can be achieved for the most difficult proteins by 2016.[[5]](#cite_note-science20201130-5) AlphaFold started competing in the 2018 CASP using an [artificial intelligence](/wiki/Artificial_intelligence "Artificial intelligence") (AI) [deep learning](/wiki/Deep_learning "Deep learning") technique.[[20]](#cite_note-:3-20)

## Algorithm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Algorithm")]

DeepMind is known to have trained the program on over 170,000 proteins from the [Protein Data Bank](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"), a public repository of protein sequences and structures. The program uses a form of [attention network](/wiki/Attention_(machine_learning) "Attention (machine learning)"), a [deep learning](/wiki/Deep_learning "Deep learning") technique that focuses on having the [AI](/wiki/AI "AI") identify parts of a larger problem, then piece it together to obtain the overall solution.[[2]](#cite_note-mittr20201130-2) The overall training was conducted on processing power between 100 and 200 [GPUs](/wiki/GPUs "GPUs").[[2]](#cite_note-mittr20201130-2)

### AlphaFold 1 (2018)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: AlphaFold 1 (2018)")]

**AlphaFold 1** (2018) was built on work developed by various teams in the 2010s, work that looked at the large databanks of related DNA sequences now available from many different organisms (most without known 3D structures), to try to find changes at different [residues](/wiki/Residue_(chemistry)#Biochemistry "Residue (chemistry)") (peptides) that appeared to be correlated, even though the residues were not consecutive in the main chain. Such correlations suggest that the residues may be close to each other physically, even though not close in the sequence, allowing a [contact map](/wiki/Contact_map "Contact map") to be estimated. Building on recent work prior to 2018, AlphaFold 1 extended this by estimating a probability distribution for the distances between residues, effectively transforming the contact map into a distance map. It also used more advanced learning methods than previously to develop the inference.[[21]](#cite_note-21)[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### AlphaFold 2 (2020)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: AlphaFold 2 (2020)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/AlphaFold_2.png/330px-AlphaFold_2.png)](/wiki/File:AlphaFold_2.png)

AlphaFold 2 performance, experiments, and architecture[[23]](#cite_note-nature-23)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Architectural_details_of_AlphaFold_2.png/330px-Architectural_details_of_AlphaFold_2.png)](/wiki/File:Architectural_details_of_AlphaFold_2.png)

Architectural details of AlphaFold 2[[23]](#cite_note-nature-23)

The 2020 version of the program (**AlphaFold 2**, 2020) is significantly different from the original version that won CASP 13 in 2018, according to the team at DeepMind.[[24]](#cite_note-economist20201130-24)[[25]](#cite_note-KahnLessons-25)

AlphaFold 1 used a number of separately trained modules to produce a guide potential, which was then combined with a physics-based energy potential. AlphaFold 2 replaced this with a system of interconnected sub-networks, forming a single, differentiable, end-to-end model based on pattern recognition. This model was trained in an integrated manner.[[25]](#cite_note-KahnLessons-25)[[26]](#cite_note-block_diagram-26) After the neural network's prediction converges, a final refinement step applies local physical constraints using energy minimization based on the [AMBER](/wiki/AMBER "AMBER") force field. This step only slightly adjusts the predicted structure.[[27]](#cite_note-Alpha2Abstract-27)

A key part of the 2020 system are two modules, believed to be based on a [transformer](/wiki/Transformer_(machine_learning_model) "Transformer (machine learning model)") design, which are used to progressively refine a [vector of information](/wiki/Word_embedding "Word embedding") for each relationship (or "[edge](/wiki/Connectivity_(graph_theory) "Connectivity (graph theory)")" in graph-theory terminology) between an [amino acid residue](/wiki/Amino_acid_residue "Amino acid residue") of the protein and another amino acid residue (these relationships are represented by the array shown in green); and between each amino acid position and each different sequences in the input [sequence alignment](/wiki/Sequence_alignment "Sequence alignment") (these relationships are represented by the array shown in red).[[26]](#cite_note-block_diagram-26) Internally these refinement transformations contain layers that have the effect of bringing relevant data together and filtering out irrelevant data (the "attention mechanism") for these relationships, in a context-dependent way, learned from training data. These transformations are iterated, the updated information output by one step becoming the input of the next, with the sharpened residue/residue information feeding into the update of the residue/sequence information, and then the improved residue/sequence information feeding into the update of the residue/residue information.[[26]](#cite_note-block_diagram-26) As the iteration progresses, according to one report, the "attention algorithm ... mimics the way a person might assemble a jigsaw puzzle: first connecting pieces in small clumps—in this case clusters of amino acids—and then searching for ways to join the clumps in a larger whole."[[5]](#cite_note-science20201130-5)[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*]

The output of these iterations then informs the final structure prediction module,[[26]](#cite_note-block_diagram-26) which also uses transformers,[[28]](#cite_note-28) and is itself then iterated. In an example presented by DeepMind, the structure prediction module achieved a correct topology for the target protein on its first iteration, scored as having a GDT\_TS of 78, but with a large number (90%) of stereochemical violations – i.e. unphysical bond angles or lengths. With subsequent iterations the number of stereochemical violations fell. By the third iteration the GDT\_TS of the prediction was approaching 90, and by the eighth iteration the number of stereochemical violations was approaching zero.[[29]](#cite_note-AF2iterations-29)

The training data was originally restricted to single peptide chains. However, the October 2021 update, named AlphaFold-Multimer, included protein complexes in its training data. DeepMind stated this update succeeded about 70% of the time at accurately predicting protein-protein interactions.[[30]](#cite_note-30)

### AlphaFold 3 (2024)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: AlphaFold 3 (2024)")]

Announced on 8 May 2024, **AlphaFold 3** was co-developed by Google DeepMind and [Isomorphic Labs](/wiki/Isomorphic_Labs "Isomorphic Labs"), both subsidiaries of [Alphabet](/wiki/Alphabet_Inc. "Alphabet Inc."). AlphaFold 3 is not limited to single-chain proteins, as it can also predict the structures of [protein complexes](/wiki/Protein_complex "Protein complex") with [DNA](/wiki/DNA "DNA"), [RNA](/wiki/RNA "RNA"), [post-translational modifications](/wiki/Post-translational_modification "Post-translational modification") and selected [ligands](/wiki/Ligand "Ligand") and [ions](/wiki/Ion "Ion").[[31]](#cite_note-31)[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)

AlphaFold 3 introduces the "Pairformer," a deep learning architecture inspired by the transformer, which is considered similar to, but simpler than, the Evoformer used in AlphaFold 2.[[15]](#cite_note-:8-15) The Pairformer module's initial predictions are refined by a [diffusion model](/wiki/Diffusion_model "Diffusion model"). This model begins with a cloud of atoms and iteratively refines their positions, guided by the Pairformer's output, to generate a 3D representation of the molecular structure.[[14]](#cite_note-:0-14)

The AlphaFold server was created to provide free access to AlphaFold 3 for non-commercial research.[[32]](#cite_note-32) As of November 2025, the AlphaFold 3 research paper has been directly cited more than 9,000 times.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### CASP13

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: CASP13")]

In December 2018, DeepMind's AlphaFold placed first in the overall rankings of the 13th [Critical Assessment of Techniques for Protein Structure Prediction](/wiki/Critical_Assessment_of_Techniques_for_Protein_Structure_Prediction "Critical Assessment of Techniques for Protein Structure Prediction") (CASP).[[34]](#cite_note-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Guardian2018-35)

The program was particularly successfully predicting the most accurate structure for targets rated as the most difficult by the competition organisers, where no existing [template structures](/wiki/Threading_(protein_sequence) "Threading (protein sequence)") were available from proteins with a partially similar sequence. AlphaFold gave the best prediction for 25 out of 43 protein targets in this class,[[35]](#cite_note-Guardian2018-35)[[36]](#cite_note-36)[[37]](#cite_note-37) achieving a median score of 58.9 on the CASP's [global distance test](/wiki/Global_distance_test "Global distance test") (GDT) score, ahead of 52.5 and 52.4 by the two next best-placed teams,[[38]](#cite_note-38) who were also using deep learning to estimate contact distances.[[39]](#cite_note-39)[[40]](#cite_note-40) Overall, across all targets, AlphaFold 1 achieved a GDT score of 68.5.[[41]](#cite_note-:2-41)

In January 2020, implementations and illustrative code of AlphaFold 1 was released [open-source](/wiki/Open-source_software "Open-source software") on [GitHub](/wiki/GitHub "GitHub").[[42]](#cite_note-42)[[20]](#cite_note-:3-20) but, as stated in the "Read Me" file on that website: "This code can't be used to predict structure of an arbitrary protein sequence. It can be used to predict structure only on the CASP13 dataset (links below). The feature generation code is tightly coupled to our internal infrastructure as well as external tools, hence we are unable to open-source it." Therefore, in essence, the code deposited is not suitable for general use but only for the CASP13 proteins. The company has not announced plans to make their code publicly available as of 5 March 2021.

### CASP14

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: CASP14")]

In November 2020, DeepMind's new version, AlphaFold 2, won CASP14.[[43]](#cite_note-DeepMindAlpha2-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44) Overall, AlphaFold 2 made the best prediction for 88 out of the 97 targets.[[7]](#cite_note-AlQuraishiTweet-7)

On the competition's preferred [global distance test](/wiki/Global_distance_test "Global distance test") (GDT) measure of accuracy, the program achieved a median score of 92.4 (out of 100), meaning that more than half of its predictions were scored at better than 92.4% for having their atoms in more-or-less the right place,[[45]](#cite_note-45)[[46]](#cite_note-46) a level of accuracy reported to be comparable to experimental techniques like [X-ray crystallography](/wiki/X-ray_crystallography "X-ray crystallography").[[24]](#cite_note-economist20201130-24)[[8]](#cite_note-:5-8)[[41]](#cite_note-:2-41) In 2018 AlphaFold 1 had only reached this level of accuracy in two of all of its predictions.[[7]](#cite_note-AlQuraishiTweet-7) 88% of predictions in the 2020 competition had a GDT\_TS score of more than 80. On the group of targets classed as the most difficult, AlphaFold 2 achieved a median score of 87.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Measured by the [root-mean-square deviation](/wiki/Root-mean-square_deviation_of_atomic_positions "Root-mean-square deviation of atomic positions") (RMS-D) of the placement of the alpha-carbon atoms of the protein backbone chain, which tends to be dominated by the performance of the worst-fitted outliers, 88% of AlphaFold 2's predictions had an RMS deviation of less than 4 [Å](/wiki/Angstrom "Angstrom") for the set of overlapped C-alpha atoms.[[7]](#cite_note-AlQuraishiTweet-7) 76% of predictions achieved better than 3 Å, and 46% had a C-alpha atom RMS accuracy better than 2 Å,[[7]](#cite_note-AlQuraishiTweet-7) with a median RMS deviation in its predictions of 2.1 Å for a set of overlapped CA atoms.[[7]](#cite_note-AlQuraishiTweet-7) AlphaFold 2 also achieved an accuracy in modelling surface [side chains](/wiki/Side_chain "Side chain") described as "really really extraordinary".

To further validate AlphaFold 2, the conference organizers approached four leading experimental groups working on structures they found particularly challenging and had been unable to determine. In all four cases the three-dimensional models produced by AlphaFold 2 were sufficiently accurate to determine structures of these proteins by [molecular replacement](/wiki/Molecular_replacement "Molecular replacement"). These included target T1100 (Af1503), a small [membrane protein](/wiki/Membrane_protein "Membrane protein") studied by experimentalists for ten years.[[5]](#cite_note-science20201130-5)

Of the three structures that AlphaFold 2 had the least success in predicting, one was an unusual [multidomain complex](/wiki/Protein_domain#Multidomain_proteins "Protein domain") consisting of 52 identical copies of the same [domain](/wiki/Protein_domain "Protein domain"). For all targets with a single domain, excluding only one very large protein and the two structures determined by NMR, AlphaFold 2 achieved a GDT\_TS score of over 80.

### CASP15

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: CASP15")]

In 2022, DeepMind did not enter CASP15, but most of the entrants used AlphaFold or tools incorporating AlphaFold.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

## Reception

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Reception")]

AlphaFold 2 scoring more than 90 in [CASP](/wiki/CASP "CASP")'s [global distance test](/wiki/Global_distance_test "Global distance test") (GDT) is a great achievement in [computational biology](/wiki/Computational_biology "Computational biology").[[5]](#cite_note-science20201130-5)[[8]](#cite_note-:5-8) [Nobel Prize](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") winner and [structural biologist](/wiki/Structural_biology "Structural biology") [Venki Ramakrishnan](/wiki/Venki_Ramakrishnan "Venki Ramakrishnan") called the result "a stunning advance on the protein folding problem",[[5]](#cite_note-science20201130-5) adding that "It has occurred decades before many people in the field would have predicted. It will be exciting to see the many ways in which it will fundamentally change biological research."[[43]](#cite_note-DeepMindAlpha2-43)

AlphaFold 2's success received wide media attention.[[48]](#cite_note-CASP_release-48)[[43]](#cite_note-DeepMindAlpha2-43)[[49]](#cite_note-49) News pieces appeared in the science press, such as *[Nature](/wiki/Nature_(journal) "Nature (journal)")*,[[8]](#cite_note-:5-8) *[Science](/wiki/Science_(journal) "Science (journal)")*,[[5]](#cite_note-science20201130-5) *[MIT Technology Review](/wiki/MIT_Technology_Review "MIT Technology Review")*,[[2]](#cite_note-mittr20201130-2) and *[New Scientist](/wiki/New_Scientist "New Scientist")*,[[50]](#cite_note-50)[[51]](#cite_note-51) and the story was covered by national newspapers.[[52]](#cite_note-52)[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) A frequent theme was the ability to predict protein structures based on the constituent amino acid sequence, expected to have benefits in the life sciences—accelerating drug discovery and enabling better understanding of diseases.[[8]](#cite_note-:5-8)[[56]](#cite_note-56) Some have noted that even a perfect answer to the protein *[prediction](/wiki/Protein_structure_prediction "Protein structure prediction")* problem still leaves questions about the protein *[folding](/wiki/Protein_folding "Protein folding")* problem (and thus [protein dynamics](/wiki/Protein_dynamics "Protein dynamics"))—understanding in detail how the folding process actually occurs in nature (and how sometimes they can also [misfold](/wiki/Proteopathy "Proteopathy")).[[57]](#cite_note-57)

In 2023, [Demis Hassabis](/wiki/Demis_Hassabis "Demis Hassabis") and [John Jumper](/wiki/John_M._Jumper "John M. Jumper") won the [Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences](/wiki/Breakthrough_Prize_in_Life_Sciences "Breakthrough Prize in Life Sciences")[[19]](#cite_note-:7-19) as well as the [Albert Lasker Award for Basic Medical Research](/wiki/Albert_Lasker_Award_for_Basic_Medical_Research "Albert Lasker Award for Basic Medical Research") for their management of the AlphaFold project.[[58]](#cite_note-58) Hassabis and Jumper proceeded to win the [Nobel Prize in Chemistry](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Chemistry "Nobel Prize in Chemistry") in 2024 for their work on "protein structure prediction" with [David Baker](/wiki/David_Baker_(biochemist) "David Baker (biochemist)") of the University of Washington.[[18]](#cite_note-:4-18)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

## Source code

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Source code")]

Open access to source code of several AlphaFold versions (excluding AlphaFold 3) has been provided by DeepMind in 2022 after requests from the scientific community.[[60]](#cite_note-ElPais-60)[[61]](#cite_note-61)[[62]](#cite_note-HassabisTweet_210618-62) The source code and weights of AlphaFold 3 was [made available for non-commercial use](/wiki/Source-available_software "Source-available software") to the scientific community upon request in November 2024. It became publicly available in February 2025, still retaining the non-commercial restriction.[[63]](#cite_note-63)

### Clones and derivatives

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Clones and derivatives")]

A number of AlphaFold clones have also been published, mostly with laxer license terms. Clones for AlphaFold3 include [ByteDance](/wiki/ByteDance "ByteDance")'s Protenix ([Apache 2.0 License](/wiki/Apache_2.0_License "Apache 2.0 License")),[[64]](#cite_note-64) AlQuraishi Laboratory's OpenFold-3 ([MIT license](/wiki/MIT_license "MIT license")), and Boltz-1/2 (MIT license).[[65]](#cite_note-65)

There are also clones for older versions, though they became less relevant with the open-source release of AlphaFold 1 and 2 source codes. Still relevant are models, both open- and closed-source, that include modifications to the AlphaFold architecture. For AlphaFold 2, a notable example is ESMFold from [Meta](/wiki/Meta_Platforms "Meta Platforms"), which replaces the multiple sequence alignment with the latent space of a protein language model.[[66]](#cite_note-66)

Open-source tools that complement AlphaFold have also been made. One well-cited example is ColabFold, which uses HHblits to speed up the sequence search, allowing the AlphaFold pipelines to run quickly on [Google Colab](/wiki/Google_Colab "Google Colab").[[67]](#cite_note-67)

The **AlphaFold Protein Structure Database** (AFDB), a joint project between AlphaFold and [EMBL-EBI](/wiki/EMBL-EBI "EMBL-EBI"), was launched on July 22, 2021. At launch, the database contained AlphaFold 1-predicted [models](/wiki/Protein_structure_prediction "Protein structure prediction") for nearly the complete [UniProt](/wiki/UniProt "UniProt") [proteome](/wiki/Proteome "Proteome") of humans and 20 [model organisms](/wiki/Model_organisms "Model organisms"), totaling over 365,000 proteins. The database does not include proteins with fewer than 16 or more than 2700 [amino acid residues](/wiki/Amino_acid_residues "Amino acid residues"),[[68]](#cite_note-68) but for humans they are available in the whole batch file.[[69]](#cite_note-69) AlphaFold's initial goal (as of early 2022) was to expand the database to cover most of the UniRef90 set, which contains over 100 million proteins. As of May 15, 2022, the database contained 992,316 predictions.[[70]](#cite_note-70)

In July 2021, UniProt-KB and [InterPro](/wiki/InterPro "InterPro")[[71]](#cite_note-71) has been updated to show AlphaFold predictions when available.[[72]](#cite_note-72)

On July 28, 2022, the team uploaded to the database the structures of around 200 million proteins from 1 million species, covering nearly every known protein on the planet.[[73]](#cite_note-73) The number as of 2024 is 214 million, with 26 million being duplicates (exact sequence matches) of another protein in the database. The predicted structures can differ significantly between duplicates.[[74]](#cite_note-Lau24-74)

As of 2025, the AFDB uses AlphaFold 2 for its predictions. All structures produced remain monomeric, but multimeric structures produced by other databases are linked on the page through the 3D-Beacons API. Foldseek, which provides fast and accurate structure searches, is also integrated. Information from AlphaMissense (a tool that uses AlphaFold to predict the outcome of missense mutations) is also integrated.[[75]](#cite_note-75)

### Derived databases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Derived databases")]

AlphaFill adds cofactors to AlphaFold models where appropriate. This is achieved by searching the [Protein Data Bank](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank") for similar structures and transplanting cofactors to analogous positions.[[76]](#cite_note-76) It is also linked to by UniProt.

TmAlphaFold docks AlphaFold models to biological membranes, similar to what OPM does for PDB structures.[[77]](#cite_note-Rosignoli25-77)

AFTM uses AlphaFold models to identify transmembrane regions in human proteins, similar to what PDBTM does for PDB structures.[[77]](#cite_note-Rosignoli25-77)

ChannelsDB 2.0 uses PDB or AlphaFill models to calculate the pathway a molecule may take to reach an enzyme's active site or to reach another side of a transmembrane pore.[[78]](#cite_note-78)

The AFDB is not updated with UniProt sequences chanegs. AlphaSync keeps the AFDB in sync with UniProt entry changes, generating updated structures, residue-level features and contacts. It tries to use an AFDB entry for the exact updated sequence when available and run AlphaFold 2 independently otherwise. It fills in AFDB's blank for large (> 2,700 aa) proteins and proteins with special FASTA characters such as B, Z, U or X.[[79]](#cite_note-79)

The Encyclopedia of Domains (TED) applies the domain-recognition method from [CATH database](/wiki/CATH_database "CATH database") to 188 million unique structures from the AFDB, identifying nearly 365 million domains, which is 100 million more than what sequence-based methods could identify.[[74]](#cite_note-Lau24-74)

### Unrelated AlphaFold-based databases

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Unrelated AlphaFold-based databases")]

isoform.io is a database of AlphaFold2-generated structures of proposed [splice isoforms](/wiki/Splice_isoform "Splice isoform") in the human genome. It includes information from 237,275 human transcripts. It has been used to detect errors in the mRNA predictions for a handful of genes.[[80]](#cite_note-80)

## Performance, validations and limitations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Performance, validations and limitations")]

AlphaFold has shown certain limitations.

### AlphaFold 1, 2, and AlphaFold DB

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: AlphaFold 1, 2, and AlphaFold DB")]

* AlphaFold DB provides models of individual protein chains (monomers), rather than their biologically relevant [complexes](/wiki/Protein_complexes "Protein complexes").[[81]](#cite_note-DB-Limitation-81)
* Many protein regions are predicted with low confidence score, including the [intrinsically disordered protein](/wiki/Intrinsically_disordered_protein "Intrinsically disordered protein") regions.[[82]](#cite_note-82)
* Alphafold-2 was validated for predicting effects of point mutations on structure[[83]](#cite_note-:1-83) and free energy,[[84]](#cite_note-84) with a partial success.

### AlphaFold 3

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: AlphaFold 3")]

* Across several benchmarks, AlphaFold3 has demonstrated, on average, superior performance to conventional search-based docking algorithms in predicting small-molecule–protein binding modes.[[85]](#cite_note-85)
* AlphaFold 3 version can predict structures of protein complexes with a very limited set of selected [cofactors](/wiki/Cofactor_(biochemistry) "Cofactor (biochemistry)") and co- and [post-translational modifications](/wiki/Post-translational_modification "Post-translational modification").[[86]](#cite_note-auto-86) Between 50% and 70% of the structures of the human proteome are incomplete without covalently-attached glycans.[[87]](#cite_note-87)[[81]](#cite_note-DB-Limitation-81)
* Studies have shown that although AlphaFold3 can jointly model protein–ligand co-folding, its accuracy drops markedly on test cases with low similarity to its training data—an area of particular importance for drug discovery.[[88]](#cite_note-88) Other work has found that AlphaFold is insensitive to adversarial decoys generated by altering the physicochemical properties of binding pockets, suggesting potential reliance on training-set memorization rather than genuine chemical awareness.[[89]](#cite_note-89)

### General

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: General")]

* In the algorithm, the residues are moved freely, without any restraints. Therefore, during modeling the integrity of the chain is not maintained. As a result, AlphaFold may produce topologically wrong results, like structures with an arbitrary number of knots. (The study uses AlphaFold 2.3.2.)[[90]](#cite_note-90)
* The model relies, to some extent, on co-evolutionary information from similar proteins. Therefore, it may not perform as well on synthetic proteins or proteins with very low homology to those in the training database.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Benchmarks support this limitation: when applied to naturally evolved de novo proteins, AlphaFold2 often yields low-confidence and predictor-dependent models, and protein language model–based (alignment-free) structure predictors can perform better for orphan proteins than AlphaFold2.[[92]](#cite_note-92)[[93]](#cite_note-93) More broadly, comparative analyses show that structure/disorder predictors (including AlphaFold2 and ESMFold) behave differently on de novo and random-sequence proteins than on conserved proteins, and that confidence metrics can show different relationships with predicted disorder in these sequence classes.[[94]](#cite_note-94)[[95]](#cite_note-95)
* The model's ability to predict multiple [native](/wiki/Native_state "Native state") conformations of proteins is limited.
* Proteins are inherently dynamic, and accessing multiple native conformations is often crucial for understanding their function. However, the model has limited capability to represent these alternative conformational states, particularly those that coexist or interconvert in biological environments.

## Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=AlphaFold&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Applications")]

See also: [Earth BioGenome Project](/wiki/Earth_BioGenome_Project "Earth BioGenome Project")

AlphaFold has been used to predict structures of proteins of [SARS-CoV-2](/wiki/Severe_acute_respiratory_syndrome_coronavirus_2 "Severe acute respiratory syndrome coronavirus 2"), the causative agent of [COVID-19](/wiki/COVID-19_pandemic "COVID-19 pandemic"). The structures of these proteins were pending experimental detection in early 2020.[[96]](#cite_note-96)[[8]](#cite_note-:5-8) Results were reviewed by scientists at the [Francis Crick Institute](/wiki/Francis_Crick_Institute "Francis Crick Institute") in the United Kingdom before being released to the broader research community. The team also confirmed accurate prediction against the experimentally determined SARS-CoV-2 [spike protein](/wiki/Coronavirus_spike_protein "Coronavirus spike protein") that was shared in the [Protein Data Bank](/wiki/Protein_Data_Bank "Protein Data Bank"), an international open-access database, before releasing the computationally determined structures of the under-studied protein molecules.[[97]](#cite_note-:6-97) The team acknowledged that although these protein structures might not be the subject of ongoing therapeutical research efforts, they will add to the community's understanding of the SARS-CoV-2 virus.[[97]](#cite_note-:6-97) Specifically, AlphaFold 2's prediction of the structure of the *[ORF3a](/wiki/ORF3a "ORF3a")* protein was very similar to the structure determined by researchers at [University of California, Berkeley](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley") using [cryo-electron microscopy](/wiki/Cryo-Electron_Microscopy "Cryo-Electron Microscopy"). This specific protein is believed to assist the virus in breaking out of the host cell once it replicates. This protein is also believed to play a role in triggering the inflammatory response to the infection.[[98]](#cite_note-98)
